baseline_kernel: // Naive complex SYRK treating A^H as separate transpose+conjugate
analysis: HERK: C = A * conj(A)^T, result is Hermitian
Diagonal elements are real (a * conj(a) = |a|^2)
Only compute lower triangle - upper is conjugate mirror
schedule_reasoning: ComplexTransform::kConjugate handles A^H internally
Diagonal forced real in epilogue (imag = 0)
Lower triangle only - 50% compute saved
Used in: covariance of complex signals, radar processing
concept: HERK lower fill - complex covariance matrix

baseline_kernel:
// Complex f64 HERK - 16 bytes per complex element
// Extreme bandwidth pressure
analysis: Complex f64: 16 bytes per element (2x8 bytes for real,imag)
Quantum chemistry requires full precision
Small N, large K: inner product dominated
schedule_reasoning: Tiny 32x32 tile due to 8x register pressure (complex f64)
DMMA for f64 tensor cores
Large K means many iterations - deep pipeline helps
Used in: density matrix computation, molecular orbitals
concept: HERK f64 complex - quantum chemistry density matrices

constraints.special: Right side: C = B * A where A is Hermitian

baseline_kernel:
// C = B * A where A is Hermitian (A^H = A)
// Naive: expand A to full matrix then GEMM
analysis: SideMode::kRight: C = B * A
A Hermitian: A[i,j] = conj(A[j,i])
Store only lower (or upper) triangle of A
schedule_reasoning: Right side multiplication: output shape is M×N from B
Upper fill: A's upper triangle stored, lower is conjugate
Tile loader applies conjugate when accessing lower portion
Used in: spectral methods, quantum operators
concept: HEMM right side - Hermitian operator application

constraints.special: f64 complex Hermitian for physics simulations

baseline_kernel:
// z64 HEMM - complex f64 Hermitian multiply
// Extreme precision for quantum/plasma physics
analysis: Complex f64 HEMM for: plasma physics, quantum field theory
Hermitian matrices common: Hamiltonians, density operators
Full f64 precision required for energy conservation
schedule_reasoning: 32x32 tile for complex f64 register pressure
DMMA tensor cores for f64
Left side: C = A * B where A is Hermitian
Used in: Hamiltonian evolution, Green's functions
concept: HEMM f64 complex - Hamiltonian matrix operations

constraints.special: No conjugate transforms - straight complex multiply

baseline_kernel:
// Interleaved complex: [r0,i0,r1,i1,...]
// Cannot use tensor cores efficiently
analysis: Planar layout: real[] and imag[] as separate arrays
Enables tensor cores: operate on pure-real tiles
Complex multiply: C = (Ar + i*Ai) * (Br + i*Bi)
schedule_reasoning: No conjugate transforms - identity complex multiply
Internally computes: Cr = Ar*Br - Ai*Bi, Ci = Ar*Bi + Ai*Br
4 real GEMMs fused: RR, RI, IR, II
Used in: FFT post-processing, complex neural networks

baseline_kernel:
// Complex correlation: conj(A) * B
// Explicit conjugation changes sign of imaginary products
analysis: Correlation: C = conj(A) * B, where conj flips sign of Ai
Cr = Ar*Br + Ai*Bi (note + not -)
Ci = Ar*Bi - Ai*Br (note - not +)
schedule_reasoning: ComplexTransform::kConjugate on A
Same 4 real GEMMs, but sign changes handled in epilogue
Asymmetric 128x64 tile for different A/B access patterns
Used in: signal correlation, matched filtering, beamforming

task.data_type: f16 input → f32 accumulate → f32 output

constraints.special: Keep f32 output for downstream ops

baseline_kernel:
// Pure f32 - no mixed precision benefit
// 4 bytes per element input/output
analysis: Input in f16 (2 bytes): 2x bandwidth vs f32
Accumulate in f32: numerical stability
Output in f32: feed directly to f32 ops (no conversion)
schedule_reasoning: f16 MMA with f32 accumulator - natural mode
f32 output skips f32→f16 conversion in epilogue
Useful when: output feeds f32 reduction, softmax, or loss
Common in: transformer FFN where output→GELU→f32 needed
concept: Mixed precision f16→f32 output - no final conversion

task.data_type: int8 input → int32 accumulate → int8 output

baseline_kernel:
// f32 inference - no quantization
// 4 bytes per element, no tensor core int8 benefit
analysis: INT8 inference: 1 byte per element (4x bandwidth vs f32)
INT32 accumulator prevents overflow during K reduction
Requantize in epilogue: int32 → scale → int8
schedule_reasoning: 16x8x32 INT8 MMA on tensor cores
INT32 accumulator for K=4096 products
LinearCombinationClamp: scale + clamp to [-128,127]
4x throughput vs f16, 8x vs f32

baseline_kernel:
// f64 FMA-only - no tensor cores
// 1 FMA per thread per cycle
analysis: Small 1K×1K problem - need high occupancy
DMMA tensor cores: 8x8x4 for f64
Smaller tiles for higher occupancy
schedule_reasoning: 32x32 tiny tile for high occupancy on small problem
5 stages for deep pipelining
Multiple threadblocks can run concurrently per SM
Grid size: 32×32 = 1024 threadblocks
concept: f64 small GEMM - high occupancy configuration

baseline_kernel:
// Large f64 GEMM - bandwidth bound
// 8 bytes × 3 matrices × 8K² elements = 1.5TB traffic
analysis: Huge problem: 8K × 8K × 8K = 512 TFLOP
At 19.5 TFLOP/s f64 peak: ~26 seconds compute
Memory: 8K² × 8B × 3 matrices = 1.5GB, but 8K³ × 8B × 2 = 8TB traffic
schedule_reasoning: 64x64 tile balances occupancy and reuse
4 stages for memory latency hiding
Swizzle<8> for L2 locality on large matrices
ColMajor: Fortran convention for HPC codes
concept: f64 large HPC GEMM - maximum throughput
